title: Waymarker Refresh
desc: Castle Bodhran's route marks fade faster than its visitors lose confidence.

steps.text:
Castle Bodhran remains charming right up until you need to get somewhere specific. The interior routes are marked with chalk arrows, color bands, and hanging cords because ordinary directions stop being useful the moment the floor becomes a wall and the doorway opens under your feet.

This morning the guest steward hands you a satchel of chalk sticks and a bundle of dyed marker cords. "Busy travel week," she says. "Too many boots, too much traffic, and three children who thought the arrows were part of a game. Which, to be fair, they are. But they're also how merchants reach breakfast instead of the laundry chutes."

She points down two separate corridors, each becoming unreasonable in its own way. "Repaint the public route through the main halls, or replace the hanging cords at the gravity turns in the guest wing. Either one will save me from explaining the castle to strangers before I've had tea."

options.label: Repaint the main route through the public halls
Replace the hanging cords in the guest wing

steps.text:
The main route begins conventionally enough. Then it rises up a side wall, crosses a ceiling passage, and returns to ordinary architecture as if nothing unusual has happened. You kneel wherever your current version of the floor happens to be and repaint the arrows one by one: blue for guest rooms, gold for the dining hall, white for the exit, which the steward insists should be the easiest route to follow and frequently is not.

The trick is consistency. An arrow that points "forward" in one corridor has to mean the same thing after the next turn, even when forward has rotated thirty degrees and your sense of balance is trying to renegotiate terms. By the time you finish the last segment, the chalk lines run cleanly from the entrance hall to the breakfast gallery without any gaps or contradictory directions.

The steward tests the route carrying a stack of folded towels. She follows the arrows up the wall, across the overhead passage, and back down into the hall below without breaking stride. "Good," she says, setting the towels aside. "Now the visitors will only be lost because they're visitors, not because we were careless."

options.label: Pack up the chalk and admire the restored route

steps.text:
The guest wing uses hanging cords where the architecture changes too abruptly for arrows to do the job alone. Blue knots for stairs, red tassels for room clusters, brass bells for the turn that leads back to the entry hall instead of into the wall pantry. Half of them have frayed or vanished, either worn away by hands or borrowed by children who wanted souvenirs from the strange castle.

You climb the maintenance ladder through a shaft that becomes a walkway halfway up and retie the cords one anchor at a time. Each turn has to be visible from the last one, otherwise the whole system fails. Once the new cords are in place, the guest wing begins to make a kind of practical sense - not ordinary sense, never that, but enough that a sleepy traveler could follow the blue knots to a bed and the brass bell back to the door.

As you descend, a merchant family enters the corridor and pauses at the first gravity shift. The youngest spots the new red tassel, points, and leads the rest of them confidently around the turn. The steward watches this happen and nods. "Excellent. We have upgraded from mystery to navigable eccentricity."

options.label: Leave the cords swaying in their proper places
